verses.text: Reuben and Gad had a great multitude of cattle, saw that Jazer and Gilead was a land for cattle. They came to Moses: the country the LORD smote is a land for cattle, thy servants have cattle. If we have found grace, give us this land; bring us not over Jordan. Guidance: A practical request born of practical wealth. The land east looked perfect for herds — fertile, watered, conquered. But ease can speak louder than promise. The first generation died for thinking the land too hard; this generation almost stops short because the side road looks better.

Moses: shall your brethren go to war, and shall ye sit here? Wherefore discourage the heart of Israel? Thus did your fathers at Kadesh — they turned the people back; the LORD's anger kindled — none from twenty years old saw the land save Caleb and Joshua. Ye are risen up an increase of sinful men. If ye turn from Him, ye shall destroy all this people. Guidance: Moses sees the shadow of the spies in their cattle-request. Stopping short doesn't only fail you — it discourages your brothers. The fastest way to destroy a community is to settle before the journey is done.

They came near: we will build sheepfolds for our cattle, cities for our little ones; but we ourselves will go ready armed before Israel, until we have brought them to their place. We will not return to our houses until every man of Israel hath received his inheritance. We will not inherit on the other side, because our inheritance is on this side Jordan eastward. Guidance: They hear the rebuke and immediately raise the offer. Houses behind, swords forward — last to settle, first to fight. A good answer to a sharp word: don't argue, restructure. Show with terms what you mean.

Moses: if ye go armed before the LORD to war until He hath driven out His enemies — afterward ye shall return guiltless; this land shall be your possession. If not, ye have sinned against the LORD — be sure your sin will find you out. Do that which hath proceeded out of your mouth. Guidance: Be sure your sin will find you out — a sentence that has outlived four millennia. Promises spoken in front of God are not abstract. He marks them. If you keep them, freedom; if not, the sin walks home behind you and waits at your door.

They to Moses: we will do as my lord commandeth. Our cattle, little ones, wives shall be in Gilead; thy servants will pass over armed for war before the LORD. Moses commanded Eleazar and Joshua: if they pass with you armed, the land shall be theirs; if not, they shall have possessions in Canaan. They: as the LORD hath said, so will we do. Guidance: A covenant ratified before three witnesses — Moses, the priest, the next leader. Their families stay east; their swords go west. Faith on this side of Jordan must be willing to walk through deep water on behalf of the other side.

Moses gave to Gad, Reuben, and half Manasseh, the kingdom of Sihon and Og. They built cities: Dibon, Ataroth, Aroer, Jazer, Heshbon, Elealeh, Nebo, Baal-meon — names that will sound in Israel's psalms for centuries. Jair of Manasseh took the small towns; Nobah took Kenath, named it after himself. Guidance: The east bank gets its first map. Half a tribe of Manasseh is added — a quiet expansion. Some name cities after themselves; the Bible records it without fanfare. Inheritance settled, swords still drawn — chapter 33 onward will tell whether the promise was kept.
